text: Aza Raskin, who designed infinite scroll while at Mozilla in 2006, later calculated that his invention costs humanity roughly 200,000 hours of attention every day. The mechanism is deceptively simple: by removing the natural pause of pagination — the moment a user decides whether to continue — the interface eliminates the decision point entirely. Psychologists call this 'default continuance': when stopping requires effort and continuing requires none, most people continue. Raskin eventually apologized publicly for the design, comparing it to building a slot machine into every website. He became a co-founder of the Center for Humane Technology.
question: What psychological mechanism does infinite scroll exploit, according to the passage?

options.text: Variable reward schedules that trigger dopamine release
Social comparison bias that makes users seek external validation
Default continuance, where stopping requires effort while continuing requires none
Loss aversion, which makes users fear missing new content
